211.description: This research focuses on leveraging NLP and AI techniques to automatically generate multiple-choice questions, cloze tests, and high-quality distractors for educational assessments. It aims to enhance the scalability and personalization of language learning and evaluation systems.

212.name: Automatic Personality Detection
212.description: This field focuses on inferring human personality traits from text data using psycholinguistic features and machine learning models. It leverages large-scale social media datasets to build predictive systems for traits like the Big Five.

213.name: Knowledge in Language Models
213.description: This direction investigates how pre-trained language models store, retrieve, and utilize factual and relational knowledge within their parameters, often using probing tasks and knowledge bases as benchmarks. It explores methods to enhance model knowledge through injection, augmentation, and memory mechanisms.

214.name: Knowledge Injection and Representation in LLMs
214.description: This research direction investigates how to effectively encode, retrieve, and integrate external knowledge into Large Language Models through methods like fine-tuning, retrieval-augmented generation, and memory mechanisms. It also explores the evaluation of model knowledge, addressing issues such as hallucination, factuality, and the distinction between parametric and non-parametric memory.

215.name: Text-Based Game Agents
215.description: This research focuses on developing AI agents that can understand natural language narratives, perceive game states, and generate appropriate dialogue and actions within text-based adventure environments. It combines techniques from natural language processing, reinforcement learning, and commonsense reasoning to create intelligent NPCs or autonomous players.

216.name: Grounded Dialogue and Embodied AI
216.description: This research focuses on developing agents that communicate naturally within simulated or real-world environments to achieve shared goals, often involving visual grounding and task execution. It bridges natural language processing with cognitive modeling to enable situated learning and collaborative problem-solving.

217.name: Conversational Recommender Systems
217.description: This field focuses on developing dialog systems that integrate natural language understanding with recommendation algorithms to provide personalized suggestions through interactive conversations. It leverages knowledge graphs, user memory, and dialogue strategies to enhance the relevance and context-awareness of recommendations.

218.name: Multi-Hop Reasoning
218.description: This research direction focuses on enhancing large language models' ability to perform complex, multi-step inference by integrating retrieved knowledge, structured graphs, and specialized prompting techniques. It addresses challenges such as hallucination, attention to distractors, and the need for explainable reasoning chains in open-domain question answering.

219.name: Few-shot Natural Language Generation
219.description: This research focuses on generating natural language responses with limited training data, often leveraging pre-trained models, data augmentation, or dual learning strategies to improve performance in task-oriented dialogues and stylized generation. Key challenges include controlling semantic accuracy, stylistic variation, and ensuring slot consistency under data-scarce conditions.

220.name: Causal Reasoning in NLP
220.description: This direction focuses on enabling natural language models to understand, infer, and reason about causal relationships, often using benchmarks, counterfactuals, and neuro-symbolic methods to address confounding and improve interpretability.

221.name: Entity Alignment
221.description: This research focuses on identifying equivalent entities across different knowledge graphs, often involving cross-lingual or multi-source data integration. It primarily employs graph neural networks, embedding models, and contrastive learning to resolve entity correspondences.

222.name: LLM-based Role-Playing Agents
222.description: This research focuses on leveraging large language models to create customizable, personality-consistent virtual characters capable of engaging in natural open-domain dialogues. It encompasses techniques for character alignment, evaluation benchmarks, and the analysis of anthropomorphism in AI interactions.

223.name: LLM Grounding and Attribution
223.description: This research focuses on enhancing the reliability of Large Language Models by improving their ability to cite sources, verify facts, and reduce hallucinations through retrieval-augmented generation and self-reflection mechanisms. It aims to ensure generated text is trustworthy and accurately linked to pre-training data or external knowledge.

224.name: Knowledge-Enhanced Language Modeling
224.description: This direction focuses on integrating structured knowledge from knowledge graphs into language models to improve reasoning, representation learning, and generation tasks. It explores methods like graph transformers, knowledge infusion, and joint text-graph modeling to bridge the gap between unstructured text and structured data.

225.name: Question Answering
225.description: This research focuses on developing systems that can answer questions by understanding text, reasoning over context, and leveraging external knowledge bases. It encompasses tasks like machine reading comprehension, open-book QA, and knowledge-intensive generation.

226.name: Computational Historical Linguistics
226.description: This field applies machine learning and NLP techniques to reconstruct proto-languages, decipher ancient scripts, detect cognates, and analyze sound correspondences across languages. It bridges computational methods with historical linguistics to automate tasks traditionally performed by human philologists.

227.name: Task-Oriented Dialogue Systems
227.description: This field focuses on building conversational agents capable of understanding user intents, extracting slot information, and executing specific tasks through techniques like data augmentation, paraphrase generation, and hierarchical pre-training. It aims to improve the efficiency, accuracy, and scalability of dialogue understanding and generation in practical applications.

228.name: Dialogue Systems and User Simulation
228.description: This field focuses on developing conversational agents, optimizing dialogue policies via reinforcement learning, and creating realistic user simulators for training and evaluation. It encompasses task-oriented dialogues, social chatbots, and the integration of large language models to enhance human-like interaction.

229.name: Out-of-Distribution Detection in NLP
229.description: This research focuses on developing methods to identify text inputs that deviate from the training distribution, utilizing techniques like contrastive learning, prompt engineering, and pre-trained transformers. It aims to enhance model robustness and security by detecting anomalies, out-of-domain data, and potential adversarial attacks in natural language processing tasks.

230.name: Dialectal NLP
230.description: This research direction focuses on developing NLP models that understand, generate, and evaluate diverse language varieties and dialects, addressing issues like bias and lack of standard orthography. It aims to improve the robustness and inclusivity of language technologies across different linguistic communities.

231.name: Synthetic Data Generation for LLMs
231.description: This research focuses on leveraging Large Language Models to automatically generate, annotate, and augment datasets for training and evaluating AI systems. It addresses challenges in data diversity, quality, and domain adaptation to improve model performance and efficiency.

232.name: Multiword Expression Identification
232.description: This research focuses on detecting and analyzing multiword expressions (MWEs), such as verb-noun phrases, using various NLP techniques including pre-trained language models, neural networks, and cross-lingual transfer learning. It aims to improve the recognition of both seen and unseen MWEs in diverse linguistic contexts.

233.name: Instruction Tuning and Prompt Engineering
233.description: This research focuses on optimizing how Large Language Models understand, follow, and generate responses to natural language instructions through techniques like prompt design, self-imitation learning, and instruction induction. It aims to enhance model adaptability, robustness, and performance across diverse tasks without extensive fine-tuning.

234.name: LLM Evaluation Artifacts and Biases
234.description: This research direction investigates systematic errors, selection biases, and cognitive artifacts in evaluating Large Language Models using multiple-choice formats. It aims to distinguish genuine model capabilities from spurious correlations like option order sensitivity or guessing strategies.

235.name: LLM Puzzle Solving and Reasoning
235.description: This research direction focuses on evaluating and enhancing Large Language Models' ability to solve various logic, lateral thinking, and word-based puzzles. It involves developing benchmarks, datasets, and prompting strategies to assess and improve models' reasoning capabilities under ambiguity and incomplete information.

236.name: LLM Reasoning and Planning
236.description: This direction focuses on enhancing large language models' ability to perform multi-step logical deduction, mathematical problem-solving, and task planning through techniques like Chain-of-Thought prompting, self-correction, and tool integration. It aims to improve the reliability and interpretability of AI reasoning processes.

237.name: Semantic Parsing and Frame Semantics
237.description: This research direction focuses on mapping natural language to structured semantic representations, often utilizing frame semantics and transition-based or graph-based neural models. It encompasses tasks like semantic parsing, frame disambiguation, and cross-lingual alignment of semantic frames.

238.name: Text Normalization
238.description: Text Normalization focuses on converting diverse textual representations, such as historical spellings, non-canonical forms, or code-switched data, into a standardized format to facilitate downstream NLP tasks. This field leverages various models, including neural machine translation, BERT, and sequence-to-sequence architectures, to handle lexical variations across languages and time periods.

239.name: Computational Linguistic Typology
239.description: This field applies machine learning and statistical models to analyze, compare, and predict cross-linguistic patterns in phonology, morphology, and syntax. It bridges theoretical linguistics with NLP by leveraging large-scale corpora and embeddings to uncover universal grammatical properties.

240.name: Sanskrit NLP and Computational Linguistics
240.description: This field focuses on developing computational methods for Sanskrit text processing, including word segmentation, morphological tagging, sandhi splitting, and parsing, often leveraging neural networks and traditional grammatical frameworks like Pāṇini's system. It involves creating specialized corpora, toolkits, and models to address the unique linguistic challenges of this ancient Indo-Aryan language.

241.name: Chinese NER and Word Segmentation
241.description: This research focuses on Named Entity Recognition and Word Segmentation for Chinese text, leveraging pre-trained models, lattice structures, and lexical knowledge to enhance representation and accuracy. It explores various architectures like BERT, LSTM, and Transformers to address the unique challenges of Chinese language processing.

242.name: Morphosyntactic Analysis
242.description: This research direction focuses on developing neural models and tools for morphological segmentation, part-of-speech tagging, and lemmatization across diverse and low-resource languages. It emphasizes techniques like cross-lingual transfer, contextual embeddings, and unsupervised induction to handle typological diversity.

243.name: Unsupervised and Low-Resource Machine Translation
243.description: This research focuses on developing machine translation systems for languages with limited or no parallel training data, utilizing techniques like unsupervised learning, transfer learning, and language model rescoring. It aims to bridge communication gaps in very low-resource scenarios where traditional supervised methods are infeasible.

244.name: Subword and Morphological Word Embeddings
244.description: This research focuses on improving word representations by leveraging subword units and morphological structures to handle out-of-vocabulary words and enhance performance in low-resource and morphologically rich languages. It explores various techniques to integrate subword information into embedding models for better generalization and robustness.

245.name: Large Language Model Scaling and Efficiency
245.description: This research direction focuses on understanding scaling laws, optimizing training stability, and developing parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods for large foundation models. It aims to improve model performance, reduce computational costs, and enhance adaptability across diverse tasks without full retraining.

246.name: LLM-based Machine Translation
246.description: This research direction explores the application of Large Language Models for machine translation, focusing on low-resource languages, synthetic data utilization, and domain-specific adaptations. It investigates techniques like fine-tuning, pre-training on translated data, and leveraging multilingual models to improve translation quality across diverse linguistic scenarios.

247.name: Robust Neural Machine Translation
247.description: This research focuses on enhancing the resilience of neural machine translation systems against adversarial inputs, noise, and perturbations. It explores techniques like data augmentation, adversarial training, and multi-task learning to improve model reliability in real-world scenarios.

248.name: Interactive Neural Machine Translation
248.description: This research focuses on enhancing machine translation systems through active learning, user feedback, and interactive prediction mechanisms to improve accuracy and adaptability. It explores methods like reinforcement learning, error correction, and sentiment preservation to address limitations in traditional neural MT.

249.name: Word Sense Disambiguation
249.description: This research focuses on developing methods and benchmarks to accurately determine the specific meaning of words based on their context, particularly in multilingual and machine translation scenarios. It addresses challenges like bias, low-resource languages, and the integration of weak supervision into neural models.

250.name: Active Learning for NLP
250.description: This research focuses on selecting the most informative unlabeled data to annotate, thereby improving model performance with minimal human effort in natural language processing tasks. It often integrates techniques like uncertainty sampling, self-supervised learning, and pre-trained language models to enhance efficiency.

251.name: Hierarchical Text Classification
251.description: This direction focuses on leveraging hierarchical category structures and label semantics to improve text classification, often employing graph neural networks, contrastive learning, or prompt tuning for inductive and few-shot scenarios.

252.name: Low-Resource Language NLP
252.description: This research focuses on developing NLP techniques for under-resourced languages, including indigenous, creole, and South Asian languages, addressing challenges in translation, transliteration, and corpus creation. It emphasizes ethical considerations and innovative methods to bridge the digital divide for linguistic minorities.

253.name: Transformer Interpretability and Visualization
253.description: This research direction focuses on developing methods and tools to analyze, visualize, and explain the internal mechanisms, attention patterns, and learned representations of Transformer models. It aims to make black-box deep learning models more transparent and understandable for both researchers and practitioners.

254.name: Pre-training of Language Models
254.description: This research focuses on developing and optimizing pre-training strategies for deep neural networks, particularly using masked language modeling and transformer architectures to enhance language understanding. It explores various techniques such as selective masking, loss functions, and pre-training corpora to improve model performance on downstream tasks.

255.name: Child-Directed Language Modeling
255.description: This research focuses on developing and evaluating neural language models trained on child-directed speech to simulate human language acquisition processes. It explores how small-scale data and specific training strategies can lead to models that better understand early grammatical and semantic development.

256.name: Compositionality in Neural Language Models
256.description: This research direction investigates how neural models, particularly transformers, represent and process lexical composition, syntactic structures, and semantic relationships in language. It focuses on evaluating whether these models truly understand compositional meaning or merely rely on statistical associations.

257.name: Word Embeddings and Probing
257.description: This research focuses on developing, evaluating, and analyzing word and sentence embeddings to capture linguistic properties, semantic relationships, and contextual meanings. It includes methods for probing pretrained models, creating benchmarks for rare words, and visualizing embedding spaces to understand their representational capabilities.

258.name: Multilingual Question Answering
258.description: This research focuses on developing datasets, models, and evaluation benchmarks for cross-lingual and multilingual question answering, particularly targeting underrepresented languages like Indic and Indonesian. It aims to improve the ability of NLP systems to perform open-domain and extractive QA across diverse linguistic contexts.

259.name: Low-Resource NLP
259.description: This research focuses on adapting pre-trained multilingual models and developing efficient techniques to enable natural language processing tasks in languages with limited training data. It addresses challenges such as cross-lingual transfer, domain adaptation, and the creation of specialized benchmarks for under-resourced languages.

260.name: Patronizing and Condescending Language Detection
260.description: This research focuses on developing NLP models to identify language that is patronizing or condescending, particularly towards vulnerable communities. It involves creating annotated datasets, fine-tuning pre-trained transformers, and exploring various classification strategies to detect such harmful linguistic patterns.

261.name: Multilingual Tweet Intimacy Analysis
261.description: This research direction focuses on quantifying the level of intimacy in social media posts, particularly tweets, across multiple languages. It leverages NLP techniques such as pre-trained transformers, data augmentation, and ensemble learning to predict intimacy scores from text data.

262.name: Food Safety NLP
262.description: This research direction focuses on applying natural language processing techniques to detect foodborne illnesses, extract outbreak events from news, and classify food hazards in textual reports. It leverages models ranging from traditional classifiers to large language models for tasks like risk classification, summarization, and hazard prediction.

263.name: Semantic Textual Relatedness
263.description: This research direction focuses on quantifying the semantic relationship between sentence pairs, often involving multilingual evaluation, embedding methods, and transformer-based models. It aims to develop robust predictors for determining whether texts are semantically related, similar, or unrelated.

264.name: Multilingual News Article Similarity
264.description: This research focuses on detecting semantic similarity between news articles across different languages using transformer-based models, ensemble methods, and cross-lingual techniques. It aims to improve near-duplicate detection and content assessment in multilingual news feeds.

265.name: Commonsense Validation and Explanation
265.description: This research focuses on enabling NLP models to validate statements against commonsense knowledge and generate explanations for their reasoning. It primarily involves leveraging pre-trained language models and knowledge resources to improve comprehension and generation tasks.

266.name: Sparse Autoencoders for LLM Interpretability
266.description: This research direction focuses on using sparse autoencoders to decompose the high-dimensional activations of large language models into a smaller set of interpretable features, enabling the analysis and manipulation of model internals. It aims to uncover latent concepts, improve model transparency, and facilitate steering or fine-tuning without relying on external datasets.

267.name: Human Value Detection in Arguments
267.description: This research focuses on identifying and classifying underlying human values within argumentative texts using NLP techniques. It involves developing models to detect multi-label value expressions, often leveraging transformer-based architectures and ensemble methods.

268.name: Natural Language Analogical Reasoning
268.description: This research direction focuses on enabling language models to identify, generate, and reason about analogies across morphological, semantic, and narrative contexts. It involves developing benchmarks, datasets, and methods to evaluate how well LLMs can map relationships between different concepts or situations.

269.name: Euphemism Detection
269.description: This research focuses on identifying coded language, such as euphemisms and dogwhistles, often used to convey hate speech or bias without explicit expression. It employs various NLP techniques including word embeddings, sentiment analysis, and large language models to disambiguate these terms.

270.name: Job Market NLP
270.description: This field focuses on applying natural language processing techniques to extract, classify, and analyze skills, competencies, and job requirements from resumes and job postings. It leverages models ranging from traditional classifiers to large language models to solve tasks like extreme multi-label classification and ontology alignment.

271.name: Multimodal Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis
271.description: This research direction focuses on analyzing sentiment and emotions associated with specific aspects in text and images, often leveraging cross-modal alignment and pre-trained models. It involves tasks like identifying complaints, inferring text-image relationships, and fine-grained emotion detection in social media and dialog systems.

272.name: Italian Large Language Models
272.description: This research direction focuses on developing, evaluating, and benchmarking large language models specifically tailored for the Italian language. It addresses challenges such as language understanding, generation, cultural awareness, and domain-specific tasks like mathematics and commonsense reasoning.

273.name: Product Question Answering
273.description: This research focuses on automatically answering user queries about e-commerce products by leveraging heterogeneous data sources such as product specifications, reviews, and similar product contexts. It involves tasks like intent recognition, information retrieval, and answer generation to enhance the shopping experience.

274.name: Scientific Claim Verification
274.description: This field focuses on automatically verifying the truthfulness of scientific claims using evidence extraction, reasoning, and multimodal analysis from research literature. It leverages NLP techniques, including LLMs, to detect inconsistencies and validate assertions in scientific discourse.

275.name: Hyperpartisan News Detection
275.description: This research focuses on automatically identifying news articles that exhibit extreme bias or hyperpartisan characteristics using NLP techniques. It leverages linguistic features, deep learning models, and domain adaptation to detect rhetorical bias and ideological slant.

276.name: Computational Argumentation and Misinformation Detection
276.description: This field focuses on developing NLP methods to analyze argumentative structures, detect fake news, identify controversial content, and understand ideological stances in text and multimodal data. It leverages both traditional machine learning and large language models to reason about persuasion, bias, and public opinion.

277.name: Zero Pronoun Resolution
277.description: This research focuses on identifying and recovering omitted pronouns in languages like Chinese and Japanese, often leveraging neural networks, multi-task learning, and pre-trained models to resolve anaphora in translation and dialogue contexts.

278.name: Knowledge Graph Information Extraction
278.description: This field focuses on extracting entities, relations, and attributes from text to construct or enhance knowledge graphs, often leveraging pre-trained language models and contrastive learning. It bridges natural language understanding with structured knowledge representation for tasks like scientific data extraction and biomedical ontology embedding.

279.name: Diffusion Language Models
279.description: This research direction explores using diffusion processes to generate text by modeling the transition from noise to coherent sequences, often employing simplex-based or continuous-discrete unifying frameworks. It aims to improve text generation quality, enable non-autoregressive inference, and accelerate sampling compared to traditional autoregressive LLMs.

280.name: Negotiation Dialogue Systems
280.description: This field focuses on developing AI agents capable of engaging in strategic, personality-aware, and adaptive negotiations to achieve optimal outcomes in various scenarios. It involves creating datasets, modeling opponent behaviors, and optimizing decision-making strategies within conversational frameworks.

281.name: Socially Intelligent Agents
281.description: This research direction focuses on developing AI agents capable of understanding social dynamics, mental states, and human values to effectively collaborate with humans in complex tasks like games and dialogues. It involves evaluating LLMs' sociability, reasoning, and ability to adapt to social contexts through benchmarks and game-based simulations.

282.name: LLM Ensemble and Routing
282.description: This research direction focuses on improving Large Language Model performance and efficiency through techniques like model ensembling, query-aware routing, and dynamic selection strategies. It aims to balance accuracy, cost, and latency by intelligently combining multiple models or selecting the most suitable one for specific tasks.

283.name: Curriculum Learning
283.description: Curriculum Learning is a training strategy that mimics human education by ordering training samples from easy to hard to improve model convergence and performance. It has been widely applied to various NLP tasks such as language understanding, generation, and reasoning.

284.name: Controlled Natural Language Generation
284.description: This research focuses on generating text with specific constraints such as diversity, sentiment, keywords, or structural coherence, often utilizing modular toolkits and evaluation metrics to ensure quality and controllability.

285.name: Questions Under Discussion
285.description: This research focuses on modeling discourse structure by identifying and parsing Questions Under Discussion (QUDs) to understand how information is organized in conversations. It involves developing datasets, annotation schemes, and algorithms to link indirect answers, speaker intentions, and background knowledge to these hierarchical question structures.

286.name: Emotion-Aware Dialogue Systems
286.description: This research focuses on integrating emotion recognition, generation, and management into conversational AI to enhance empathy and user engagement in both task-oriented and open-domain interactions. It involves developing datasets, annotation schemes, and models that enable systems to understand, respond to, and express emotions effectively.

287.name: Dialogue Systems and Generation
287.description: This field focuses on developing models for natural conversation, including response generation, structure learning, and robustness in multi-turn interactions without relying on parallel data. It encompasses task-oriented dialogues, chit-chat, and the integration of reinforcement learning for dynamic user feedback.

288.name: NLP Data Annotation Tools
288.description: This research direction focuses on developing software platforms and tools that facilitate the creation, management, and quality control of annotated datasets for natural language processing tasks. These tools often incorporate features like active learning, collaborative workflows, and domain-specific adaptations to streamline the human-in-the-loop data preparation process.

289.name: Biomedical NLP and LLM Applications
289.description: This field focuses on applying natural language processing and large language models to extract, retrieve, and reason over complex biomedical and clinical data. Key tasks include entity recognition, event extraction, question answering, and information retrieval from electronic health records and scientific literature.

290.name: Learning with Noisy Labels
290.description: This research focuses on developing robust training methods and noise detection techniques to improve model performance when data contains incorrect or inconsistent annotations. It addresses challenges in text classification and sequence labeling by mitigating the impact of label errors through adversarial training, self-training, and pre-trained language models.

291.name: Named Entity Recognition and Span Extraction
291.description: This research focuses on identifying and classifying specific entities (such as chemicals, legal terms, or medical concepts) and text spans within unstructured data, often addressing challenges like domain adaptation, noisy labels, and low-resource scenarios. It encompasses techniques ranging from traditional machine learning to advanced neural architectures for information extraction tasks.

292.name: LLM Evaluation and Benchmarking
292.description: This research direction focuses on developing methodologies, benchmarks, and surveys to assess the capabilities, limitations, and reliability of Large Language Models across various NLP tasks. It critically examines model performance through human expert grading, systematic evaluations, and community perspectives on intelligence and task solving.

293.name: Automatic Term Extraction
293.description: This research focuses on identifying and extracting domain-specific terminology from unstructured text corpora using various NLP techniques. It addresses challenges such as meaning shifts, cross-lingual transfer, and the integration of Large Language Models to improve precision and adaptability.

294.name: LLM Reasoning and Agents
294.description: This direction focuses on enhancing large language models' capabilities in logical, physical, and strategic reasoning through techniques like prompting, self-reflection, tool augmentation, and autonomous agent frameworks. It also involves developing benchmarks to evaluate these advanced reasoning skills in complex scientific and real-world scenarios.

295.name: Explainable and Consistent Reasoning in NLP
295.description: This research focuses on enhancing the interpretability, logical consistency, and faithfulness of natural language models through techniques like entailment trees, symbolic reasoning, and belief tracking. It aims to align model outputs with human logic and verify intermediate reasoning steps.

296.name: Dataset Evaluation and Robustness
296.description: This research focuses on characterizing datasets, generating challenging evaluation sets (e.g., contrast sets), and assessing model robustness against distribution shifts and data contamination. It aims to improve the validity and fairness of benchmarks through rigorous testing methodologies.

297.name: Question Answering
297.description: This research direction focuses on developing models and datasets to enable systems to extract answers from text, ranging from reading comprehension to generating questions and handling domain adaptation. It encompasses techniques like span selection, semantic parsing, and data augmentation to improve accuracy and robustness.

298.name: Retrieval-Augmented Generation Security
298.description: This research focuses on identifying and mitigating vulnerabilities in Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems, including adversarial attacks, privacy breaches, and robustness issues. It explores techniques to secure the retrieval and generation pipelines against malicious inputs and data poisoning.

299.name: Long-Context Question Answering
299.description: This direction focuses on developing methods to retrieve and reason over information in extremely long documents or contexts, often combining retrieval mechanisms with generative models. It addresses challenges like multi-hop reasoning, structural document understanding, and efficient search within massive token windows.

300.name: French Language Modeling and NLP
300.description: This research direction focuses on developing, adapting, and evaluating large language models and transformer architectures specifically for the French language, addressing challenges such as low-resource scenarios, noisy text, and domain-specific applications like aviation or spontaneous dialogue.

301.name: Mechanistic Interpretability of Language Models
301.description: This field focuses on understanding the internal mechanisms of large language models by identifying specific neurons, circuits, and subnetworks responsible for storing knowledge and performing tasks. It aims to make black-box models more transparent through techniques like neuron attribution, circuit discovery, and feature analysis.

302.name: LLM Evaluation and Robustness
302.description: This research direction focuses on developing rigorous methods to evaluate, benchmark, and improve the reliability, consistency, and robustness of Large Language Models through diverse prompting strategies and comparative analysis. It addresses critical challenges such as prompt sensitivity, model contradictions, and the limitations of existing leaderboards.

303.name: Efficient Text Embeddings
303.description: This research focuses on compressing and optimizing word and sentence representations to enable fast, low-resource, and on-device text processing. Key techniques include binarization, projection-based distillation, and information bottleneck methods to reduce dimensionality while preserving semantic utility.

304.name: Gamified Language Resource Creation
304.description: This research direction focuses on leveraging game-based mechanisms and citizen science to collect high-quality, naturally occurring linguistic data for low-resource languages and specific domains. It combines gamification incentives with crowdsourcing to build language resources that support NLP tasks and language learning applications.

305.name: Efficient Language Modeling
305.description: This direction focuses on optimizing large language models through techniques like vocabulary reduction, architectural simplification (e.g., MLPs), and training strategies (e.g., Sharpness-Aware Minimization) to improve inference speed, memory efficiency, and generalization without sacrificing performance.

306.name: State Space Models for Language Modeling
306.description: This research direction focuses on advancing State Space Models (SSMs), particularly Mamba architectures, to improve efficiency, long-context understanding, and reasoning capabilities in language modeling. It involves techniques like token reduction, hybrid attention mechanisms, and parameter-efficient fine-tuning to enhance SSM performance.

307.name: Named Entity Translation
307.description: This research focuses on improving the accuracy and consistency of translating proper nouns, such as names and locations, within neural machine translation systems. It employs techniques like knowledge graphs, multi-task learning, and entity-aware models to handle cross-cultural and domain-specific challenges.

308.name: Parliamentary Corpus Construction
308.description: This research direction focuses on building, annotating, and analyzing large-scale corpora of parliamentary proceedings, debates, and speeches across multiple languages and historical periods. It aims to create high-quality electronic resources for linguistic research, translation, interpretation, and political science studies.

309.name: LLM Pretraining Data Curation
309.description: This research focuses on improving language model performance by filtering, deduplicating, and curating large-scale web-crawled corpora to enhance data quality and reduce redundancy. It involves developing methods to assess text quality, detect overlaps, and construct balanced datasets for effective model pretraining.

310.name: Low-Resource and Morphologically Rich Language NLP
310.description: This research focuses on developing NLP tools, datasets, and models for languages with complex morphology (e.g., Hebrew, Polysynthetic languages) and limited digital resources, often leveraging Bible corpora and specialized pre-training techniques. It addresses challenges in tokenization, morphological analysis, and translation for historically significant or typologically unique languages.

311.name: Sequence-to-Sequence Pre-training for Language Generation
311.description: This research direction focuses on developing pre-training objectives and architectures that enable language models to generate coherent text by predicting future tokens or sequences, often using denoising, autoregressive, or multi-token completion strategies. It aims to improve the quality and efficiency of natural language generation tasks such as translation, summarization, and dialogue.

312.name: Semi-Supervised and Zero-Shot Sequence Labeling
312.description: This research focuses on developing robust text classification and sequence labeling methods that leverage limited labeled data, unlabeled data, or knowledge transfer from pre-trained models. Key techniques include data augmentation, instance selection, and adapting transformer-based architectures for few-shot and zero-shot scenarios.

313.name: Multilingual Semantic Resources and Concept Learning
313.description: This research focuses on constructing multilingual lexical resources, inducing semantic concepts, and modeling word sense disambiguation and collocations across languages. It leverages linguistic typology, bilingual dictionaries, and neural embeddings to bridge semantic gaps and enhance cross-lingual understanding.

314.name: Lottery Ticket Hypothesis in Transformers
314.description: This research focuses on identifying sparse subnetworks within pre-trained transformer models that can achieve performance comparable to the full model with significantly fewer parameters. It explores techniques for model compression, efficiency, and generalization improvement through pruning and sparse initialization strategies.

315.name: Unsupervised Cross-lingual Alignment
315.description: This research focuses on developing methods to align linguistic units (words, phrases, sentences) across different languages without parallel training data, often leveraging optimal transport, neural embeddings, or auxiliary languages. It aims to solve data sparsity and improve multilingual representation learning for tasks like translation, retrieval, and sentiment analysis.

316.name: Minimum Bayes Risk Decoding
316.description: This research focuses on optimizing neural text generation by selecting outputs that minimize expected loss according to a reference metric rather than maximizing model likelihood. It explores various strategies to improve efficiency, diversity, and quality in machine translation and general text generation tasks.

317.name: Machine Translation Quality Estimation
317.description: This research focuses on automatically predicting the quality of machine translation outputs at word or segment levels to guide post-editing and model selection. It explores methods ranging from sequence tagging and optimal transport to leveraging Large Language Models for reference-free evaluation.

318.name: Pretrained Language Model Adaptation
318.description: This research focuses on efficient methods to adapt large pretrained language models to diverse downstream tasks, including fine-tuning strategies, parameter-efficient tuning, and transfer learning techniques. It explores how to optimize model performance while managing computational costs and data limitations.

319.name: Sentence Embeddings and Text Matching
319.description: This research focuses on learning dense vector representations for sentences and paragraphs to capture semantic meaning, enabling tasks like semantic textual similarity, paraphrase identification, and text matching. It explores various architectures, pooling mechanisms, and pre-training strategies to improve the alignment and discrimination of textual data.

320.name: Efficient Transformer Architectures
320.description: This research focuses on optimizing Transformer models through techniques like attention mechanisms, parameter reduction, and training strategies to improve efficiency and scalability. It explores methods to enhance language modeling performance while reducing computational costs and model size.

323.name: Multilingual NLP for Low-Resource Languages
323.description: This research focuses on developing NLP tools, datasets, and pre-trained models specifically for Vietnamese, Thai, Danish, and other low-resource languages to address linguistic disparities. It emphasizes building open-source toolkits and evaluating performance across diverse languages beyond English.

324.name: Chat Translation
324.description: This research focuses on adapting neural machine translation models to handle the unique characteristics of bilingual multi-speaker conversations, such as dialogue history and speaker turns. It involves developing context-aware architectures, leveraging large language models, and creating specialized datasets to improve translation quality in chat scenarios.

325.name: Neural Machine Translation Hallucination
325.description: This research focuses on detecting, understanding, and mitigating hallucinations in neural machine translation systems. It explores model introspection, contrastive decoding, and evaluation metrics to improve translation reliability.

326.name: Syntax-Aware Pre-training
326.description: This research direction focuses on integrating explicit syntactic structures and grammatical relations into neural language models to enhance their understanding of linguistic hierarchy and improve cross-lingual transfer capabilities. It explores methods like syntax trees, syntactic data augmentation, and auxiliary parsing tasks to make transformers more robust and linguistically grounded.

327.name: Multilingual Large Language Models
327.description: This research direction focuses on developing, evaluating, and interpreting large language models that support multiple languages, addressing challenges such as cross-lingual transfer, language confusion, cultural diversity, and performance in low-resource settings.

34.name: Text Readability Assessment
34.description: This research direction focuses on automatically evaluating and predicting the readability or linguistic complexity of texts using computational methods, often leveraging neural networks and linguistic features.

321.name: Neural Machine Translation Enhancement
321.description: This research direction focuses on improving neural machine translation through techniques such as adversarial training, semantic integration, attention mechanisms, and leveraging pre-trained language models.

322.name: Ukrainian Language NLP
322.description: This research direction focuses on developing and evaluating natural language processing resources, models, and benchmarks specifically for the Ukrainian language, often addressing low-resource or mid-resource challenges.
